# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. what is the best one night movie?

**Score:** 60
**Body:** i mean films that take place over just one night and somehow capture all the drama tension or magic in that short time. any recommendations?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. After Hours (1985)
2. Die Hard
3. Superbad. Training Day. Dazed and Confused.
4. Dazed and Confused.
5. American graffiti (1973)
6. game night
7. Can’t Hardly Wait
8. Before Sunrise
9. Go! Was fun.     
Young Timothy Olyphant, Sarah Polley, Katie Holmes...
10. Night On Earth

## 2. the best true crime documentaries to watch

**Score:** 46
**Body:** give me your best true crime documentaries that shook you to your core, has a heartwarming ending, or keeps you up at night. 

TIA!😃

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Paradise Lost series ( I think there’s 3 and then also a full-length feature by Peter Jackson ), the Painter and the Thief, Tickled
2. The Thin Blue Line (1988)
  - The grandfather of them all. Tells a life and death story with powerful real-life reverberations for some of the people involved.

Did the reenactments thing so ingeniously and innovatively, and did it first. And the visual style of this film continues to hold its own.

Even as Errol Morris has evolved past this film’s style into further developments in works like “Standard Operating Procedure,” “Wormwood” and “Chaos,” there’s still something unique and powerful in the look and feel of his first true crime film.
3. Dear Zachary
4. The jinx
5. Devil in the Family; The fall of Ruby Franke.
6. Cocaine Cowboys is incredible. It's one of those movies I can watch over and over again. It's far more entertaining and well made than Scarface which may have inspired it most.
7. The Keepers
8. Tickled
9. Capturing the *Friedmans

* I misspelled in case anyone saw, but I guess it makes it look weird - go in blind

## 3. Looking for a spooky movie that’s not scary

**Score:** 17
**Body:** If that makes sense. I don’t like horror, but I like spooky. Maybe something that’s more psychological, something deep, well written. No jumpscares, no gore, but something that fits in with like October/ Halloween 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Others (2001)

The Sixth Sense (1999)
2. Stir of Echoes
3. I loved A Ghost Story. But it’s a very artistic film with a lot to grapple with. Some other suggestions might be The Crucible, The Others, or The Sixth Sense. They are all scary in their own right but have a deeper story to tell.
  - A ghost story is one of my favorite movies. I might rewatch it
4. I just watched Night Tide from 1961. It's a surreal stylized folk-horror about a sailor, played by Dennis Hopper, who falls in love with the boardwalk carnivals mermaid. But is she just a pretty girl who puts on a fin and lays in an aquarium or is there more to her origins. It's a really well-made early indie film that has lots of the weirdness, mystery, and takes your mind to the strange places that a horror will while there ain't any blood or jump scares. It's on tubi.
5. Try The Lady in White. It seems like it's going to be scary, but it's more of a mystery/suspense movie.
6. The ghost and Mr chicken
7. What Lies Beneath (2000)
8. Late Night with the Devil (2023)\
Let the Right One In (2008)(Swedish)\
The Innocents (2021)(Norwegian)\
The Others (2001)
9. I’m not 100% sure it will fit your category but I really liked The Vast of Night. 

It doesn’t have any jumpscares and it definitely leaves a lot of the spookiness to your imagination. Also the sound design is insanely good

## 4. Looking for supernatural horror movies

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Hi, I am looking for supernatural horror movies. I am looking for movies based in a house/room with wierd stuff happening there or someone got possessed. I have seen and loved insidious, conjuring series, sinister, autopsy of jane doe. Didnt like the others, hereditary, the exorcist(1973). 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Peter Jackson's braindead
  - What a mean thing to say about a talented director!

/s

I actually wasn’t aware of Braindead. I’ve seen the Frighteners though and it’s one of my favorites.
2. Poltergeist (1982)
3. 'Session 9' (2001)

'The Changeling' (1980)
4. 1408

The Shining
5. What Lies Beneath, The Omen, Poltergeist
6. The Thing(1982) is what you are looking for.
7. “The Haunting” (1963 black & white version) is scary and there is no monster
8. Paranormal Activity
9. Devil (2010)

## 5. Need recs: Movies/series that are actually good… but also kinda hot

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Okay, here’s the deal: I enjoy movies and shows that actually have quality writing, acting, and storylines — but also don’t shy away from erotic scenes. Think Love (Gaspar Noé), Blue Is the Warmest Color, Elite, etc.
Now I’m stuck — I feel like I’ve already watched the “obvious” ones.
So here’s my question for you:
👉 What’s a movie or series that surprised you by being both really well-made and unexpectedly hot?
Bonus points if it’s something most people wouldn’t immediately think of.
Let’s see who drops the most underrated gem.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **Normal People** (HULU) is both excellent and hot 🔥
- Following Marianne and Connell, from different backgrounds but the same small town in Ireland, as they weave in and out of each other's romantic lives and start to grow up

**Tell Me Lies** (HULU) is pretty good and addictive and definitely hot. 
- Lucy Albright and Stephen DeMarco quickly fall into an addictive entanglement that will permanently alter their lives and the lives of everyone around them; a tumultuous and intoxicating relationship unfolds over eight years.

**The Handmaiden** (2016) excellent in every way! 
- With help from an orphaned pickpocket (Kim Tae-ri), a Korean con man (Ha Jung-woo) devises an elaborate plot to seduce and bilk a Japanese woman (Kim Min-hee) out of her inheritance

**The Dreamers** (2003) very good and with more than a little sexy weirdness. 
- In May 1968, the student riots in Paris only exacerbate the isolation felt by three youths: an American exchange student named Matthew (Michael Pitt) and twins Théo (Louis Garrel) and Isabelle (Eva Green). Having bonded over their mutual love of cinema, Matthew is fascinated by the sense of intimacy shared by Isabelle and Theo, who were born conjoined. When the twins' bohemian parents go away for a month, they ask Matthew to stay at their place, and the three lose themselves in fantasy.

**Secret Things** (2002) it's a love it or hate it experience with the plot but it's definitely hot. 
- When Sandrine (Sabrina Seyvecou), a bartender at a strip club, loses her job for refusing to have sex with a customer, she moves in with stripper Nathalie (Coralie Revel), who teaches her how to use her sexuality to win over men. Together, the two find work at a bank, determined to sleep their way to the top. While Sandrine destroys the life of middle-aged executive Delacroix (Roger Mirmont), Nathalie is drawn to Christophe Bernay (Fabrice Deville), the depraved son of the firm's dying founder
2. Sense 8

Banshee
3. Outlander
  - Thank you 
Can you suggest more movies?
4. Malena. If a guy hasn't seen this one, their life is incomplete
5. BOUND
6. Some old chestnuts in here but if you have seen them, try:  
The Lover  
Lust, Caution  
Secretary  
The Handmaiden  
Call Me By Your Name  
Sleeping With Other People  
Newness
7. Misfits - especially seasons 1-3


Black Sails
8. Unfaithful (2002)

A series: I Love Dick (Prime)
9. Try **The Handmaiden** buddy. With beautifully shot, amazing storyline and definitely delivers on the heat. Chec also **Normal People** also surprised me with how tender but intense it was

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. What is a great movie that didn't click for you?

**Score:** 3346
**Body:** Yea it's a great movie but just didn't care for it.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I HAVE ABANDONED MY CHILD
  - I DRRRRINK YOUR MILKSHAkE
    - DRAINNNAGE
  - I'VE ABANDONED MY BOY (meg)
2. I guess he didn't drink your milkshake
  - DRAAAAAINNNNAGE ELI
3. The brutalist just like, nothing made sense to me. Hell of an acting by Brody tho
4. The Irishman. I forced myself to make it halfway through and couldn't take it anymore. It was hyped up beyond belief, but just doesn't do anything for me.
  - It’s sooooo bad. Distracting CGI, weird performances. A total misguided effort. We don’t NEED a de-aged Robert Deniro. Just cast a REAL YOUNG human person.
5. Oppenheimer. Not my cup of tea.

## 2. Just Peak

**Score:** 2016
**Body:** Matthew McConaughey's Performance in this was peak

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. S1 of True Detective might just be ***THE*** greatest bit of television ever produced.
  - It's in a dead tie with Chernobyl for me. I suppose they can both be in first since Chernobyl is a miniseries and not a multi-season show.
  - S1 of True Detective and The Wire are on tier of their own.
2. *the world needs bad men....we keep the other bad men from the door*
3. Woody ain’t bad his own self…I should rewatch that season.
  - Halfway through s2, I love Ferrel and McAdams but it’s just not cutting it. I heard s2 dips and s3 brings it back.
4. True Detective, The Wire & Chernobyl are all peak TV and part of why I prefer series to films. They are that good I have watch multiple times…. Shout out to Band of Brothers too
5. Can we take a second to appreciate the COLOR GRADING and cinematography in this season? It's god damn phenomenal. I noticed in some shots where Woody is being interviewed you can see individual strands of fabric with natural contrast, as well as a smooth palette across the entire scene. 

This show is a masterclass in color grading.

I've researched it with a couple people, and every time they are all amazed by the story, mystery, music and the cinematography.

Easily top 3 TV shows of all time.
6. 1st season always peak
  - Read something once along the lines of 

“Writers have their entire lives to create season 1, then often have one year to create season 2”

## 3. "Con Air" is one of my guilty pleasures. Anyone else feel the same? John Malkovich was freaking amazing in this movie.

**Score:** 813
**Body:** [Cyrus "the Virus" ](https://youtu.be/w029IJ1S7eE?t=15)was such a classic 90's action movie character. Everyone loves Nic Cage in this movie (he was a freaking bad-ass in the movie too, no doubt), but I loved John Malkovich's character so much. The guy is such a phenomenal actor, regardless of what role he is in. That line he dropped to Danny Trejo's character when he was about to take advantage of that female prison guard was so unbelievably and surprisingly noble (given his characters extremely violent nature), and absolutely hilarious: "Do you fly, Johnny? No? You keep that in mind when you look at her. Because if your dick jumps out of your pants, you jump off this plane." Classic.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Con air is brilliant, Armageddon is brilliant , the rock is brilliant anyone pretending they aren’t is a fucking snob and I’m
Editing my comment to add Under siege….. and Die hard with a vengeance is as good as possibly better than the first
2. I re-watch it every July 14th
  - I get that reference. Lol. Cheers
3. EVERYBODY is freaking amazing in this movie, from Trejo to Meaney, from Cage to Buscemi, from sea to shining sea.
4. Love this movie. Chapelle is great and Steve B is just the perfect level of creepy. Also love the part where he explains they are listening to a song made famous bc the band died in a plane crash.
5. Same vein as Face Off which is ridiculous but so much fun.
6. Fuck guilty man, Con Air pride forever
7. 🎶HOW DO I LIIIIIIIIIVE WITHOUT YOUUUUUUUU 🎵
8. Love it. 

“He's to land at Carson City Airport as scheduled.”

“Carson City? The law is down there. You lost your mind?”

“According to my last psych evaluation, yes.”
9. If you want to watch con air, you have to do it proudly, son

## 4. Which actor?

**Score:** 634

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. OJ Simpson - The Naked Gun
2. Ashton Kutcher in Butterfly Effect
3. Jared Leto / Blade Runner 2049 and hopefully soon Tron Ares.
4. At this point, most Kevin Spacey roles.
5. I despise Tom Cruise, but I loved Edge of Tomorrow
  - Tom Cruise is entertaining as hell on-screen. Apparently he's also great to work with.


Just the wholr Scientology thing..
    - My wife works in vfx and he would come by the office and hang with the vfx artists and be super interested in all the nerdy stuff they were doing. The man is really sincere and loves filmmaking. It’s so weird - like is that the real him? Or is the cult tom the real him? Is the sincerity and kindness an affect? Or is he so deep in the cult that he compartmentalizes? Creepy.
  - Les Grossman
6. My wife hates Angelina Jolie but loves Wanted.
7. Mark Wahlhbeurg in *The Departed*. I refuse to learn how to spell his name.

## 5. Which actor had the best career after failing as a leading man or lady? For me it has to be Barry Pepper because after Battlefield Earth he never got a lead role in a movie again but has had a career that has stacked up with some of the greats.

**Score:** 325

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’ll always remember him for that sniper scene in Saving Private Ryan.
  - Dude was a badass in that movie.  Straight badass
  - He's great in everything.
  - Throwing out bible quotes with each pull of the bolt, and that sudden change when he sees the tank zero in on the tower…
2. Barry is a great actor. My favorite movie of his is 61*. He literally became Roger Maris for me.
3. Taylor Kitsch has managed to carve out a really solid career after John Carter and Battleship (and Savages) not working out. Since, he's managed to have some really solid supporting roles in film and also arguably still lead roles in television - I think he's currently the lead in a The Terminal List Prequel Series as we speak.
  - Taylor Kitsch sort of has the Brad Pitt thing of being an incredible supporting actor stuck in a leading man’s body. I’m glad Kitsch has revitalized his career, because he’s a very talented actor.
  - I really enjoyed him in American Primeval
4.  I don't know if I would consider his career as the leading actor a failure, but Ed Harris's career was certainly more successful as a supporting actor.
5. Pepper was great in 25th hr

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. One Battle After Another has one of the finest car chases in any movie.

**Score:** 72
**Body:** In this thread, all pretexts of critical analysis, sociopolitical commentary, intertextuality with Pynchon, etc. are out. I’m just here to gush about the climactic chase between Willa, Bob, and the Christmas Adventurer bozo. Somehow, P.T. Anderson found an original way to shoot a car chase. 

What really struck me about the chase was its simplicity and elegance. After 90 minutes of frenetic inter-cutting and Cuarón-esque long tracking shots, Anderson pares the scene down to Hitchcockian essentials. Because Smith has already shot Lockjaw off the road, we know what his plan is and how close he needs to get to Willa before he can kill her. The focus is on the topography of the road, the cars passing in and out of each others’ sight lines, and the closing distance between them. 

The vertical shot compositions are spectacular, of course, but just as important is the way the hilly landscape creates compositional depth. There are three distances the characters have to follow: the road zooming by in front of them (shown in dizzying hubcap-level shots), the hill ahead in the middle distance, and the cars drifting in and out of view as they crest each hill. These three points of reference are all changing at different rates, creating a wobbly, vertiginous effect. At the same time, the cars are closing on on each other slowly enough that there’s an eerie sense of temporary, unstable equilibrium.

Subtler than the cinematography, but maybe just as important, is the editing. I’d be fascinated to know the tempo of shot lengths. On a first viewing, though, it seemed to me as if the shots were of roughly consistent length throughout the chase. The ordinary logic would be accelerating tempo = building tension. But the choice to keep the pace at an even keel is brilliant, because it means the viewer has to rely on the same cues Willa does. Her strategy of leaving her car on the road is all the more satisfying because we understand how tricky it would be for her to time it out. 

It bums me out that fans of action movies and thrillers probably aren’t going to see *OBAA* in droves. Considering how bloated and cluttered big-budget action movies have gotten, I think this scene, among others, would impress a lot of casual cinemagoers who might not otherwise watch a PTA film. There’s a lot to be said for making a chase scene as big and elaborate as possible, in the tradition of *Bullitt*, *The French Connection*, and *Raiders of the Lost Ark*. That tradition reached its zenith with *Mad Max: Fury Road*—a film that, come to think of it, shares a lot of narrative and thematic common ground with *OBAA*. But this chase was such a wonderful reminder of what you can do with the basic visual grammar of movement. 

Questions for discussion:
 
1. What were your thoughts on this scene? Or, more generally, how *OBAA*  manages to tackle revolutionary leftist politics, inter-generational tragedy, the American police state, and the pathology of racism while still being, to quote Wikipedia, an “action thriller film”? 

2. Have any of you had the chance to see *OBAA* in a 1.43:1 IMAX showing? First of all: [Congrats. Happy for you. Nice.](https://knowyourmeme.com/memes/congrats-happy-for-you) Second, I’m desperate to know how this scene, along with the rest of the film, looks with more vertical space from the 70mm negative. 

EDIT: Typos, corrected character name.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I kept thinking how brilliant it was while watching it. Never seen a chase on a road with rolling hills like that before, how that changes the dynamic completely. You can’t go too fast or you’ll fly over a hill and crash. Your line of sight is constantly being impeded. It was simple and brilliant. I image PTA on a drive in such terrain one day and just thinking “this would make for an interesting chase” and voila
  - IIRC the car chase in Bullitt had a similar dymanic, only using the hilly streets of San Francisco instead of a barren desert road. It similarly played with line of sight this way.
  - That’s exactly how PTA came up with the idea. They were scouting locations in CA and drove the highway. And he came up with the idea at that time.
2. Heads up, Avanti is not the person who shoots Lockjaw. Avanti is the person that Lockjaw hands Willa over to. 

Agree with everything else, though. Even the getaway from the bank somehow felt more grounded than most car chases in other films. It seemed like their lives were genuinely at risk, and I chalk that up to the verisimilitude of the filmmaking. You could feel the crunch of the crash!
  - Thanks! Embarrassed I misread the cast list. Of course that character’s name is fucking Tim Smith. XD 

I especially loved the overhead shot of Perfidia running into the parking lot.
    - I kept waiting for Perfidia to get run over. It's so stressful.
  - > Even the getaway from the bank somehow felt more grounded than most car chases in other films. 

Yeah, agreed! All the car sequences really struck me as feeling very real and stressful, and every car crash was brutal.
3. Saw yesterday, on IMAX this scene was like a rollercoaster simulator. 

Fantastic and elegant visuals and storytelling all at once.. Willa showing she’s her own hero, outsmarting these supremacist fucks, the scene is action but taps into some zen flow state place with the wave like movements.. loved it 

Regina Hall said in an interview that she asked Paul ‘how on earth you did a single-lane car chase?’ And she’s right , one of the most pleasantly suprising parts of the film’s last act.
4. Saw it in IMAX and the chase was absolutely breathtakingly awesome to behold. 

By design I knew very little about the movie beforehand, only the cast and the fact that it was ultimately bound to be another PTA masterclass. I really enjoyed the ride. Great film
5. I was definitely thinking about BULLITT during the chase. It's without doubt a brilliant chase sequence.

I think the sheer derangement of the muscle cars careening around parabolic hillsides in the San Francisco countryside might shade this one ... plus the fact McQueen was doing his own driving.

## 2. How did you feel about the use of the colour blue in Blue Is the Warmest Colour?

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I loved the movie overall, but felt the use of blue was just bolted on in various places. It also doesn’t seem to be used consistently to highlight one particular aspect, but rather appears all over the place, seemingly just to draw attention.

I’m not super deep into movies or art in general, so I’m a bit lost on whether there are ‘standard’ interpretations of things like this that others might have noticed, which I missed.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I felt it was used very warmly

[Placeholder text so I can reach the word limit. Look at me write these words. There sure are a lot of them. Man I wrote a good amount of words. Did you know I wrote a good amount of words? Okay, that should be enough words. At least I hope so.]
2. I don't know how much you can draw out of the film as "art" or of thematic importance when it turn out it was mainly a vehicle for the director to get two attractive young women to roll around naked for a full week+.


It's a shame because there were ingredients for greatness with the cast involved. But hey, exploiters gonna exploit.
  - Tbf it can be simultaneously true that elements of the film (such as the color blue) have thematic and artistic intent put into them and the director was also using the movie as a vehicle for exploiting actresses. These aren’t necessarily mutually exclusive with one another.

## 3. ONE BATTLE AFTER ANOTHER and the neurotic replay of the western left

**Score:** 0
**Body:** It's an oversized portion of great fun, and unlike its arguable counterpart ONCE UPON A TIME ... IN HOLLYWOOD, it makes a staunch effort to land on the right side of history as well as being very, very funny. 

Alongside its propulsive comedy chase caper, the film's _a luta continua_ message also reproduces the self-undermining reflexions of what are now several generations of marginal, militant western left-wing politics, each with its real cohort of ageing and dissolute revolutionaries. VINELAND was published in 1990, 35 years ago, when radical groupuscules were already fit for purpose materials for a farcical stoner dad comedy.

Despite its merits and its palpable kindness, does the film offer its audience a properly generative thought about the pump action of the United States' historical machinery? Or does its inward-looking American romanticism point to the futility of another battle waged on these terms? 

https://letterboxd.com/attentive/film/one-battle-after-another/

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I thought about those issues right after watching the movie. I liked it a great deal, though, let me be upfront about it.

But yeah, i would say, just like most American contemporary narratives about revolution, it's mostly defeatist. The only viable alternative at the end of it all is, essentially, "go to protests more". Which, fair enough.

One thing i'll say, though, is that it tried to avoid the tried-and-true cliche of "revolution bad because violence". Perfidia's actions are never explicitly condemned as wrong per se, although her moral character is called into question when she gives up the names of her colleagues.

I don't know, as i said, it's mostly defeatist and the story seems to resolve mostly as an emotional background for Di Caprio and his character's "daughter".
  - I feel like the part about "Perfidia's moral character being questionable for ratting out" is reconciled by the end of the movie with the letter Willa receives. Perfidia's betrayal is paralleled several times throughout the movie, e.g. billy goat being captured, the lady at the nunnery confessing she would talk if she had the info, the non-binary kid from school giving up willa's phone. These were depicted very matter-of-factly and could be read as the movie saying betrayal is part of the fight and fascists will always threaten your comrades to betray you, but there's only so much you can do as a revolutionary, especially if you're extra vulnerable to fascist violence due to your circumstances, and blame shouldn't be placed on the individual. My take on Willa's resentment towards Perfidia being a rat was more of a projection for her being absent from her life.
  - I'm glad we had some of the same feelings about this. I feel anyone who's spent considerable time on political activism might do the same.

There's a strong sense in which I share the ambient defeatism, and that's the sense in which the film depicts mostly methods of resistance that are well-worn and carry the aura of romantic failure. Maybe this is just my preexisting feeling the film shares in, rather than one it enforces. A lot of reviews seem to regard it as a powerful intervention. 

Benicio as Sergio was among the film's most positive political notes for me. His character stands in for competent organising achieving something very concrete and immediately crucial in US politics. And he's surrounded by well-prepared and effective allies. 

I agree with you that despite spreading out a broad sweep of political history and geography, the film willfully contracts its arc down to its family drama. >!To such an extent you're led to feel the rationality of Perfidia's decision to walk away from Bob and Willa, and not just sympathise with the post-natal depression the film indicates is gripping her!<, considering the film seems to deny itself autonomy by sticking to that frame.

---
